articles.title: Baby Pine Martens Return to England
articles.summary: Pine martens are small animals like weasels that were gone from England for over 100 years because people hunted them. Now, conservationists are helping them come back to places called Dartmoor and Exmoor in south-west England. They released dozens of pine martens into secret spots in autumn 2024. In July, it was exciting news that the pine martens had babies, called kits. The project leader, Tracey Hamston, says more kits should be born now, and they hope to see them on cameras in May and June. The pine martens wear collars to track them, and some have traveled far from where they were released. People are asked to report if they see a pine marten to help with tracking. This project helps bring these animals back to the wild.

articles.title: A Trip to the Moon: Following Astronauts on a Big Adventure
articles.summary: This article is about a reporter who followed a space mission called Artemis II for 10 days. Four astronauts, Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen, traveled to the Moon and back in a rocket. The reporter watched from Florida as the rocket launched with a bright light and loud noise. The astronauts lived in a small capsule like a minibus, with no privacy, and used a special toilet that cost a lot of money. They saw Earth from space and said it looked beautiful. The mission was exciting and historic because humans went further into space than ever before.

articles.title: How Eating Times Can Help You Stay Healthy
articles.summary: A new study found that when you eat might help you keep a healthy weight. Scientists looked at over 7,000 adults and saw that two habits are linked to a lower body mass index (BMI), which is a way to measure if someone is a healthy weight. First, having a longer overnight fast, which means not eating for more hours at night. Second, eating breakfast early in the morning. The research was done by a health institute in Barcelona. They think eating earlier fits better with our body's natural clock, helping us burn calories and control hunger. But they say more studies are needed before giving advice. The study also found differences between men and women, with women generally having lower BMI but sometimes feeling more stressed. Some men who ate late and skipped breakfast didn't see weight benefits. This is part of a field called chrononutrition, which looks at when we eat, not just what we eat.

articles.title: Scientists Find New Air Pollution in Oklahoma
articles.summary: Scientists from the University of Colorado Boulder were studying tiny particles in the air in Oklahoma when they found something surprising. They discovered a type of toxic chemical called Medium Chain Chlorinated Paraffins (MCCPs) in the air for the first time in the Western Hemisphere. These chemicals are used in making things like metalworking fluids, PVC, and textiles. The scientists think the MCCPs might come from fields where a special fertilizer made from sewage sludge is used. This fertilizer can release harmful compounds into the air. MCCPs are similar to other chemicals that are already regulated because they can travel far and harm health. The scientists used a special instrument to measure the air for a month to make this discovery. They hope to learn more about this pollution to keep people safe.

articles.title: Scientists Prove an Old Idea About Vitamin B1
articles.summary: Scientists have proven a 67-year-old idea about vitamin B1, which is a vitamin that helps our bodies work. They found a way to make a very unstable molecule called a carbene stable in water. Carbenes are special carbon molecules that usually break down quickly, but the scientists used a protective shield to keep them safe. This discovery shows that vitamin B1 might form carbenes in our cells to help with important reactions. It could also lead to cleaner ways to make medicines and other products, using water instead of harmful chemicals. The research was done by chemists at UC Riverside and published in a science journal.

articles.title: Your Nose Can Warn About Alzheimer's Early
articles.summary: Scientists found that losing your sense of smell might be an early sign of Alzheimer's disease, even before memory problems start. This happens because the brain's immune cells, called microglia, mistakenly attack nerve fibers that help you smell. These fibers connect two important brain parts: the olfactory bulb, which processes smells from your nose, and the locus coeruleus, which helps control smell. The research used studies on mice and humans, including brain scans and tissue analysis, to show this link. This discovery could help doctors detect Alzheimer's sooner and start treatments earlier, like with special antibodies, to help people better. The study was published in a science journal and done by researchers in Germany.
